coredata.dc:title: Chapter 5 Natural Biomass as Carbon Sources for the Synthesis of Photoluminescent Carbon Dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Synthesis, Technology and Applications of Carbon Nanomaterials
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2019 Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Jamaludin, Norhanisah
Rashid, Suraya Abdul
Tan, Tongling
coredata.dc:description: Photoluminescent carbon dots (CD), one of the nanomaterial family members with a size of <10nm, have become increasingly popular in the past decade due to its unique optical and electric properties. Semiconductor quantum dots that were developed in the earlier years consist of toxic heavy metal elements in its structure, thus rendering it to be applied in environmental and biological application. Due to this drawback, CD acts as promising alternatives to overcome this shortcoming. Over and above, CD presented low toxicity, high biocompatibility, ease of functionalization, and tunable luminescence. Respectively, CD has shown a great potential to be widely applied in various application. In this chapter, a brief summary of CD background was discussed. The use of natural carbon-based materials as a starting material and different approaches on the preparation of CD were summarized. A summary of CD structure and properties was also discussed briefly.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Biomass
Biochar
Top-down approach
Bottom-up approach
Photoluminescence

Biochar as Carbon Source Starting Material for Carbon Dots Preparation
Preparation of Carbon Dots via Hydrothermal Method
Morphological Properties of Biochar Derived Carbon Dots
Optical Properties of Biochar Derived Carbon Dots
Challenges and Future Directions in Synthesis of Carbon Dots

ce:figure.ce:label: Fig. 5.1

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The depicture of CD preparation from different carbon-based materials.

ce:figure.ce:source: (Reproduced with permission from Y. Song, S. Zhu, B. Yang, Bioimaging based on fluorescent carbon dots, RSC Adv. 4 (2014) 27184–27200.)

ce:figure.ce:label: Fig. 5.2

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Structures of different carbon nanoparticles according to the years of discovery.

ce:figure.ce:source: (Reproduced with permission from P. Namdari, B. Negahdari, A. Eatemadi, Synthesis, properties and biomedical applications of carbon-based quantum dots: an updated review, Biomed. Pharmacother. 87 (2017) 209–222.)

ce:figure.ce:label: Fig. 5.4

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The two approaches in synthesis of carbon dots.

ce:figure.ce:source: (Reproduced with permission from B. De, N. Karak, Recent progress in carbon dot–metal based nanohybrids for photochemical and electrochemical applications, J. Mater. Chem. A 5 (2017) 1826–1859.)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration of steps in preparing carbon dots from biochar.
FESEM images of biochar from coconut shell (A), kenaf (B), and EFB (C).
Comparison of FTIR spectra between coconut shells (A), kenaf (B), and EFB (C) biochar.
HRTEM images of coconut-shell (A)-, kenaf (B)-, and EFB (C)-based CD.
Comparison of photoluminescence under ambient light (A) and under UV lamp (B) at temperature 250°C. From left to right: coconut-shell-based, kenaf-based, and EFB-based CD.
Photoluminescence spectrum of coconut-shell (A)-, kenaf (B)-, and EFB (C)-based CD at temperature 250°C.
Comparison of CD properties derived from various types of carbon sources and synthesis process

chapter.ce:label: Chapter 5
chapter.ce:title: Natural Biomass as Carbon Sources for the Synthesis of Photoluminescent Carbon Dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Materials Processing and Technology Laboratory, Institute of Advanced Technology, Universiti Putra Malaysia, Serdang, Malaysia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Advanced Technology, Universiti Putra Malaysia

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical and Environmental Engineering, Faculty of Engineering, Universiti Putra Malaysia, Serdang, Malaysia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical and Environmental Engineering, Faculty of Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photoluminescent carbon dots (CD), one of the nanomaterial family members with a size of <10nm, have become increasingly popular in the past decade due to its unique optical and electric properties. Semiconductor quantum dots that were developed in the earlier years consist of toxic heavy metal elements in its structure, thus rendering it to be applied in environmental and biological application. Due to this drawback, CD acts as promising alternatives to overcome this shortcoming. Over and above, CD presented low toxicity, high biocompatibility, ease of functionalization, and tunable luminescence. Respectively, CD has shown a great potential to be widely applied in various application. In this chapter, a brief summary of CD background was discussed. The use of natural carbon-based materials as a starting material and different approaches on the preparation of CD were summarized. A summary of CD structure and properties was also discussed briefly.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research is partially supported by the Ministry of Higher Education Malaysia (MOHE) and Universiti Putra Malaysia via research grant FRGS (5524981) and IPB Putra (9515400). The authors would like to thank Pakar Go Green Sdn Bhd for providing biochar samples and Institute of Advanced Technology, Universiti Putra Malaysia, for the laboratory assistance.

ce:section.ce:para: Over the past decades, the discoveries in changes of physicochemical properties of materials once they are prepared in nanoparticle form have gathered significant interest among researchers. In this respect, physicochemical properties of nanoparticles are ultimately determined by the size of nanoparticles. The impact of size-dependent effects on the optical properties of nanomaterials, with emphasis on photoluminescence, is highlighted [1]. Photoluminescence is a process that involves absorption of energy and subsequent emission of light by the irradiation of a photoluminescent material with ultraviolet light. In general, photoluminescence involves two processes: excitation and emission. Photoluminescence occurred when the material is excited by ultraviolet radiation and later released energy in the form of photon during the emission process [2].

Semiconductor nanomaterials, namely, quantum dots (QD) (e.g., cadmium selenide (CdSe) and cadmium telluride (CdTe)) [3], have pulled in a tremendous measure of enthusiasm in the course of recent decades due to their excellent photoluminescent properties. Unfortunately, these inorganic semiconductor QD contain carcinogenic elements that are generally toxic and hazardous to humans and other living organisms in the environment. This has limited their applications to devices and sensors, yet their eventual discharge to the environment has known to be detrimental. Therefore, it is very desirable to look for organic photoluminescent nanomaterials that are nontoxic and can be produced via a simple, green, scalable, and economical synthesis process [4]. Organic CD are mainly derived from carbonaceous materials such as activated carbon, biochar, and coal. The advantages of these organic carbon dots (CD) are that they are just as chemically stable, yet their inherent organic nature renders them environmentally and biologically compatible. The wavelength-tunable emission properties of CD have made this nanomaterial as a promising “nanolight” for biological applications.

All forms of organic materials have carbon nanostructures that contributed tremendously in the progress of nanomaterial development [5]. The development of nanostructured allotrope forms of carbon began with the discovery of three-dimensional graphite and then one-dimensional carbon nanotubes (CNT) in 1991 by S. Iijima [6] and the discovery of another allotrope carbon, two-dimensional graphene, by Novoselov, Geim, and coworkers in 2004 [7]. To date, carbon-based nanomaterials with their excellent optical and electrical properties and potential applications have attracted much attention as potential competitors to conventional semiconductor QD. CD, a zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterial, is gradually becoming a shining star in the research field due to its potential as a nontoxic alternative for semiconductor-based QD [8]. CD is a widely used term for various kinds of nanosized carbon materials. Generally, all carbon materials having nanosized particles of <10nm [9] can be referred as CD. Fig. 5.1 illustrates CD derived from different carbon sources via various routes of synthesis. CD are quasi-spherical nanoparticles and usually made up of sp2 carbon atoms and sp3 carbon atoms at edge planes with the attachment of surface functional groups (COOH and OH groups). These attractive carbon nanostructures with the outstanding optical properties contribute in exhibiting luminescence properties such as photoluminescence (PL), chemiluminescence (CL), and electrochemiluminescence (EL) [10]. The unique optical properties displayed by CD are also caused by quantum confinement and edge effects [11, 12]. Inspired by these superior advantages, a variety of synthesis approaches have been developed to prepare CD. Notably, the success of synthesizing CD by utilizing wastes as starting materials has been an eye-opener for researchers on how to convert wastes into valuable and usable products.

As illustrated in Fig. 5.2 , CD was first discovered after separation and purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNT) using gel electrophoresis methods by Xu et al. [13] in 2004. During the research, two new nanomaterials were discovered: luminescent carbon nanoparticles and short tubular carbon. In spite of that, not much attention was given to the luminescent nanoparticles until the research work carried out by Sun's research team [14] hit the limelight 2 years later. These luminescent nanoparticles are now well known as CD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure and Properties of Carbon Dots
ce:section.ce:para: Generally, the size of CD is largely dependent on the preparation methods but independent of the starting materials. Different particle sizes of CD can be obtained by tailoring the reaction temperature and time [15,16] during the synthesis of CD. For example, Pan et al. use GO as starting material in order to produce two different kinds of CD with average sizes of 9 and ~3nm, respectively, by applying different thermal reduction temperatures and hydrothermal deoxidation alkalinities [17,18]. Other than the size, the height of CD is also dependent on the preparation method. The height of CD prepared via the same method will increase upon the increasing size of CD. Technically, CD is only composed of elements C and H. Nonetheless, limited by their strong tendency for aggregation due to face-to-face attraction and the preparation method such as oxidation-derived exfoliation method, CD that has been reported is always partially oxidized with hydroxyl, epoxy/ether, carbonyl, and carboxylic acid groups on its surface structure [19,20]. Thus, Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) are commonly selected to analyze the elements inside CD. Any presence of CO, CO, CC, and COOH bonds indicates the functionalization of CD with hydroxyl, carbonyl, and carboxylic acid groups. This functionalization has enabled CD to become more soluble in water and make it easier to modify the surface structure of CD.

Although there are lots of research discussing on the structure of CD, the mechanism of CD formation is still an open debate. Thus, a deeper understanding regarding the preparation of CD and mechanism involved, either the mechanism of CD formation or luminescence mechanism, needs to be studied by constructing ongoing research works [20]. CD exhibits several outstanding characteristics that can distinguish it from semiconductor QD: (1) Most of the CD produced up to now usually displays light absorption around 230–320nm in the ultraviolet region. Yet, the emission wavelength may vary along the visible region depending on the carbon sources and synthesis routes chosen. (2) Just like the traditional QD, CD also display a continuous and broad excitation spectrum in which different emission wavelengths are emitted under the excitation of just a single wavelength. This enables the researchers to observe the possibilities of simultaneous detection of multiple components of biomolecules [18,21]. (3) The intensity of CD luminescence is difficult to be reduced in continuous excitations due to its high photoluminescence intensity and resistance to photobleaching [22]. (4) The luminescence intensity of CD is tunable by adjusting the condition of the synthesis routes during the preparation of CD [23–25]. (5) Certain CD shows the properties of upconverted photoluminescence (UCPL) where it emits a shorter luminescence wavelength under a longer excitation wavelength [26,27]. As reported by Shen et al. [28], when the excitation wavelength is about 600–800nm, the UCPL wavelength shifted to 390–468nm after their CD was passivated with PEG. (6) Other than photoluminescence, CD also showed electrochemiluminescence properties like semiconductor QD [29]. Besides, in comparison with semiconductor QD, CD mainly consist of elements carbon, oxygen, hydrogen, and nitrogen in return resulting in low toxicity and good biocompatibility [30,31].

Despite the diversity of the structures, CD also possess similar optical properties in terms of their absorption and luminescence. As mentioned earlier, CD shows a strong optical absorption in the UV region of 230–320nm with a tail extending to the visible range. For the carbon core, a maximum peak at 230nm is ascribed to the π-π⁎ transition of aromatic CC bonds, whereas a shoulder at 300nm is attributed to the n-π⁎ transition of CO bonds or other connected groups [32]. Moreover, the chemical groups found on the CD structure may contribute to the light absorption at the UV-visible regions. To some extent, the observed deviations in absorption spectrum data indicate the differences either in the compositions or structures of different hybridization derivatives of CD. In terms of determining the PL mechanism and applying CD for any novel applications, it is crucial to study the PL properties of CD first. In general, CD exhibits emission spectra that are almost symmetrical on the wavelength scale. In comparison with the emission peaks by organic dyes, the emission peaks exhibited by CD are usually wide. This emission peak position is always influenced by the excitation wavelength, which is known as wavelength-dependent behavior. This behavior could be a result from the wide distribution of various-sized dots and surface chemistry, from different emissive traps (salvation effect), or even from the unresolved mechanism [33].

Applications of CD in various fields can be categorized based on its properties as illustrated in Fig. 5.3 . By achieving certain optical properties needed, CD can be applied in the dye-sensitized solar cell (DSSC) and supercapacitor. On the other hand, by tuning its electric properties, CD can be applied in both biological and chemical sensors. Meanwhile, the application of CD in photocatalysis can be done by tailoring both optical and electrical properties. As a result, CD shows great potential in a variety of applications as luminescent nanomaterials with biocompatibility and low toxicity.

Solar energy conversion by photocatalyst has received tremendous attention as a promising sustainable technology to overcome the energy and environmental problems resulting from the depletion of fossil fuel, pollution, and global warming [34]. Photocatalysis is based on the light absorption by the semiconductor such as zinc oxide (ZnO), titanium dioxide (TiO2), and cadmium sulfide (CdS) to excite the electrons from the valence band to the conduction band. This excitation will then leave a hole in the valence band and an excess of negative charge in the conduction band, hence creating a photogenerated electron-hole pair that can trigger the photoredox reactions [35–37]. However, present photocatalytic system suffers from the low usage of sunlight and high recombination rate of photoinduced charge carriers, which limits the quantum efficiency of photocatalysis. For photocatalysis, there are three main factors in determining the efficiency of photocatalysis: broadband absorption ability, excellent electron transfer, and photoredox properties [38]. As reported by Zhang et al., the enhancement of electron transfer properties of CD was done using petroleum coke as its raw material with the introduction of strong surface groups. [39]. To date, much research on CD-based photocatalysis with improved solar-to-energy conversion efficiency has been reported [40–44]. For instance, CD has shown a good capability in separating the photogenerated electron-hole pairs and hence enhanced the adsorption capacity in composite photocatalysis systems [45,46]. In addition, CD shows excellent optical absorption properties in both ultraviolet (UV) and visible region [41].

Apart from performing as a photocatalyst, CD shows remarkable merits as promising photosensitizer in photocatalytic system. DSSC has attracted attention as a renewable source of energy due to its high performance, low cost, and simple fabrication process [47–49]. DSSC is based on the adsorption of organic molecular dyes by the sensitization of mesoporous, nanocrystalline metal oxide films to visible light. Back in 1991, dye-sensitized solar cell with optically active sensitizing material like ruthenium (Ru)-based dye was introduced. Ru-based dye was used to produce a low-cost and efficient solar cell [50]. As Ru is belonged to the transition-metal group, there is a concern in health hazard aroused. Thus, Ru-based dye was replaced by a new type of light-absorbing material, namely, CD. CD has unique optoelectronic characteristics such as size-dependent tunable energy bandgap and carrier multiplication due to its quantum confinement [51–53]. Thus, the photovoltaic performance and stability of DSSC can be enhanced by utilizing these promising characteristics of CD.

In the above mentioned photocatalytic and photosensitizer applications of CD, it has been found that CD can also act as a supercapacitor. Supercapacitor has received attention in energy storage field due to its high power density and storage capacity, long cycle of life, environment-friendliness, light weight, and small size [54]. Generally, supercapacitor can be categorized into two classes: (1) electric double-layer supercapacitor (EDLS) and (2) pseudocapacitor. In ELDS, carbon-based materials such as graphene and GO are mainly used due to their good conductivity, high specific surface area, and long-cycle stability [54]. Meanwhile, in pseudocapacitor, transition-based metal oxides [55,56] and conducting polymer [56–60] redox active material are used due to its high capacitance behavior [61]. Graphene has been shown to be a promising electrode material in supercapacitors; however, the strong π-π interactions between graphene result in the agglomeration of the sheets, and thus, the sizes of the pores are reduced for allowing the access of electrolytes. To overcome this problem, CD with sizes below 10nm usually consist of nanocrystalline core with major sp2-hybridized carbon and amorphous shell that contain surface functional groups such as hydroxyl, epoxy/ether, and carbonyl groups, making them promising materials in supercapacitors [43,62–64].

Photoelectrochemical (PEC) sensor based on luminescent nanomaterials is an excellent detection tools for biological substance, for instance, in the detection of glutathione. This is due to its advantages such as lower limits of detection, lower potential consumption, easy process, and easier extension to light addressable sensors for multichannel detection [65–67]. The usage of semiconductor QD containing heavy metals has limited the application of PEC in biological detection due to its toxicity and high limitation of detection and does not exhibit a good enough sensing performances [65]. Therefore, a new luminescent nanomaterial that is CD has been utilized in developing PEC as it has low environmental risk, unique optical properties, high chemical stability, and excellent biocompatibility [68]. A lot of research has focused on semiconductor QD biosensors due to their novel properties [69]. Nonetheless, the toxicity of semiconductor QD has limited its application in clinical analysis. Thus, there has been a growing interest among researchers to develop a nontoxic, biocompatible, and highly luminescent nanoparticles for biological application. Herein, CD as one of the promising classes of luminescent nanoparticle has come through as it displays a low toxicity and environmentally friendly characteristics. Also, CD only require a facile steps and are easy to be prepared in a large scale [62]. Due to these advantages, a progress in applying CD as optical labels in biological labeling, bioimaging, and biomedical has been achieved [70].

As expected, a large amount of publications on CD in different fields can be found in the literature, indicated that increasing attention has been paid to these luminescent nanoparticles. Up to now, several methods have been reported in synthesizing CD, which include pyrolysis [23], acidic oxidation [71], laser ablation [72], hydrothermal treatments [73], and microwave/ultrasonic passivation [74]. Unfortunately, these methods have disadvantages where toxic reagents are used, high reaction temperatures are needed for extended lengths of time, and tedious processing steps are required. These drawbacks have limited the application of the synthesis [75]. Therefore, new methods via green synthesis have been developed where natural carbon materials such as soy milk [73], egg [76], garlic [74], coffee grounds [77], honey [78], and prawn shell [79] were used as a carbon source for the synthesis of carbon dots. CD can be obtained from different types of carbon sources going through different approaches. These various approaches are normally categorized as “top-down” and “bottom-up” as represented in Fig. 5.4 . For top-down approach, it involves the breaking down of bulk materials into a much smaller dimension of nanoparticles. Meanwhile, bottom-up approach offers the opportunities to control the nanoparticles of photoluminescent carbon dots with well-defined size, shape, and properties [80]. However, this approach involves complex synthetic procedures and special organic precursors that are quite difficult to be obtained [81].

In top-down approach, the nanoparticles are obtained by breaking down relatively large starting materials into desired nanostructure and sizes. In general, CD can be obtained by breaking down the epoxy bond that is attached to the edge of chemical structure of carbon source. Examples of carbon sources reported using top-down approaches are CNT, graphite powder, carbon rods, carbon fibers, and even candle soot [82–85]. All of these carbon-based materials possess sp2 carbon structures. Thus, the size and surface chemistry of the materials have to be modulated. The most common top-down approach is chemical oxidation method with the use of highly concentrated sulfuric or nitric acids in the reaction [86]. As a result, the bulk carbon materials are then cut down into smaller pieces, and the surfaces of these pieces are then modified by the oxygen-based groups or oxidation process resulting in small carbon products known as CD.

Other top-down approaches include electrochemistry [29,87], hydrothermal/solvothermal method [17,19], and metal-graphite intercalation [88]. In electrochemistry process, the structure of graphite rod electrode is broken down during the electrochemical cutting process to form CD. The applied electrolyte usually contains ethanol [40], ionic liquid [87], monosodium phosphate (NaH2PO4) [22], tetrabutylammonium perchlorate (TBAP) [20], phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) solution [89,90], and so on. The electric field then peels off the nanosized carbon from the electrode through intercalation of graphite layer or radical reaction. On the other hand, in hydrothermal/solvothermal methods, the oxidized carbon materials that possess defect-based chemical groups or oxygen-based groups are broken down into small pieces under high temperature and pressure provided [17,19].

In bottom-up approach, the CD are formed by assembling small precursor molecules like citrate [91] or ascorbic acid [92] using different thermal treatments such as pyrolysis, hydrothermal carbonization, or microwave-assisted methods. The usage of biomass sources such as chitosan or lignocellulosic materials as precursor materials can also be classified into the same synthesis strategies [93]. There are several approaches that have been reported for carrying out dehydration, polymerization, and carbonization in synthesizing CD such as hydrothermal pyrolysis in concentrated acids [94,95], microwave/ultrasonic passivation [15], combustion method [96], carbonization in microreactor [97], and modified hydrothermal method [98,99]. The process seems the same as in top-down approaches, yet it uses different types of starting precursor materials and modified steps. Normally, the applied starting precursor will consist of OH, COOH, CO, and NH2 functional groups that are able to dehydrate at elevated temperatures [100].

In the work reported by Mao et al., CD obtained from unscented candle soot [101] using acid reflux, centrifugation, and electrophoresis process result in water-stable, small carbon dots with sizes of <2nm that emit different colors under UV light source. Meanwhile, CD prepared via a facile, high-output hydrothermal method using citric acid and ethylenediamine has also been reported by Yang et al. [102]. On the other hand, synthesis of CD using microwave-assisted approach gives out advantages in terms of simplicity of process, low cost in production, shorter reaction time, and ability to generate variety of CD in just one step. As reported by Zhu et al., CD were prepared using microwave pyrolysis by mixing PEG200 and sugar into water in order to form a transparent solution and subsequently heated in the microwave for several minutes [96]. Following Zhu's work, Liu et al. proposed a one-step microwave process in synthesizing water-soluble CD using citric acid and urea solution [30]. In 2015, Sun et al., reported a microwave-assisted synthesis of CD, co-doped with nitrogen and phosphorus resulting in dual luminescence emission and a high quantum yield [103].

Traditional semiconductor QD are mostly derived from compounds like CdSe, lead (II) sulfide (PbS), or any heavy metal constituents. However, their practical applications are somewhat limited due to biotoxicity, environmental concerns, and high cost issues [104]. As compared with conventional QD, CD have attracted great interest among the scientific community as it avoids environmental and health concerns arising from the toxicity of heavy metal ions [105]. As a result, many researchers have come out with alternatives by using natural carbon-based materials such as wastes and biomass instead of heavy metals for the preparation of CD.

CD have been synthesized by various methods including ball milling and ultrasonication [106], pyrolysis [99,107,108], chemical oxidation and degradation [109–112], hydrothermal method [81,113–120], electrochemical release [121], biogenic synthesis [122], and thermal oxidation [123]. In top-down approach, pyrolysis and chemical oxidation are mainly carried out for the synthesis of CD, whereas hydrothermal method is mainly used in the bottom-up approach. All of the synthesis methods are using various kinds of carbon sources such as food and wastes [81,99,107,108,110–117,120–123], carbon nanopowder [111], anthracite [106], activated carbon [112], MWCNT [121], and also chitosan [118]. Among them, the average size of CD obtained is between 1 and 10nm that meets the characteristic of CD that is having size below 10nm. In terms of PL properties displayed by each CD, excitation wavelength used is in the range of 300–450nm and emitting wavelength in the range of 350–530nm. Table 5.1 provides a detailed comparative study for the synthesis of CD from various types of starting materials through different synthesis methods.

As discussed in earlier section, there are many studies that have reported the preparation of CD from various types of carbon sources using both top-down and bottom-up approaches. Thus, in this section, preparation of CD using biochar that has never been reported before is further discussed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biochar as Carbon Source Starting Material for Carbon Dots Preparation
ce:section.ce:para: Biochar is defined as a carbon-rich material produced during pyrolysis process that is a thermochemical decomposition of biomass with a temperature about ≤700°C in the absence or limited supply of oxygen [124]. Biochar is usually produced as a cost-effective and environmentally friendly sorbent. The ability of biochar in stabilizing organic and mineral compounds is due to its physicochemical properties such as porous structure, expanded specific surface area, high organic carbon content, active functional groups, and also high cation-exchange capacity (CEC) [125,126]. Pyrolysis is usually the method of choice to produce biochar, though biomass gasification produces a smaller char yield compared with pyrolysis. The production of biochar using pyrolysis process is influenced by the source of biomass, the properties (particle size and moisture content) and composition (contents of cellulose, lignin, and ash) of biomass, and also the process parameters (temperature, heating rate, pressure, and residence time) [127]. In order to produce a high char yield, a low-temperature and low-heating-rate process is more preferred [128]. The characteristics of biochar are mainly determined by the type of biomass used and the parameters of pyrolysis process. By knowing the effect of each parameter on the biochar properties, the production of biochar with a desired performance can be tailored. As reported by Angin, temperature is the only pyrolysis parameter that can control the elemental compositions of biochar and their atomic ratios [129]. The type of biomass chosen to undergo pyrolysis process plays an important role in determining the status of biochar. Biomass chemical compositions and its size, shape, and structure can significantly affect the sorption capacity of biochar [124]. Agricultural wastes that are locally available can be considered as a low-cost and renewable pyrolysis feedstock [130]. By converting this biomass into the biochar, the residues are able to be utilized in a sustainable way.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Carbon Dots via Hydrothermal Method
ce:section.ce:para: CD can be derived from biochar using a facile and green hydrothermal method whereby no harsh chemicals are used. In this top-down method of preparation, biochar is initially ground into a fine powder. For small amounts or lab-scale preparation, grinding can be done using simple pestle and mortar, whereas for bigger-scale operations, ball milling is a feasible approach. The hydrothermal method can be achieved by dispersing biochar into a water-alcohol solution and exposing the mixture to subcritical water temperatures ranging from 250 to 350°C. The length of time it takes for the biochar powder to be broken down and converted into CD depends on the heating method. Conventional ovens require at least 45min for the process to take place, whereas faster heating methods such as using salt baths or microwave ovens may only require 5min for the conversion. The vessels used in the heating process are very simple, and although autoclaves can also be used, studies have shown that the vessel need not be pressurized in order for conversion to take place. After the heating process, a black suspension is obtained, and simple centrifugation will yield a clear supernatant that consists of the CD. Typical conversion percentages of biochar into CD range from 30 to 50% depending on the hydrothermal process parameters such as water-solid ratio, temperature, and processing time. A simple illustration of the facile method of CD derivation from biochar is shown in Fig. 5.5 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological Properties of Biochar Derived Carbon Dots
ce:section.ce:para: The following description describes the properties of CD derived from three types of biochar: coconut shell, kenaf, and EFB. Herein, CD is prepared through hydrothermal process using salt bath at subcritical water temperature of 250°C for only 5min. Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) analysis was used to identify the morphology for the three types of biochar. Fig. 5.6 showed that the surface of the biochar observed was quite similar between the three types of biochar. It appeared as small carbon particles that are different in sizes with a certain degree of agglomeration due to the ball milling process. The agglomeration of particles can be explained as single, large particle that was surrounded by many other smaller particles. Yusof mentioned that some of the particles had edges and some were round due to the abrasion that caused a loss of facets [131].

The infrared spectral analysis was used to determine the possible functional groups that are responsible for the photoluminescence properties of the samples. The analysis was performed on all three types of biochar used: coconut shell, kenaf, and empty fruit bunch (EFB). The major peaks in Fig. 5.7 observed for coconut shell, kenaf, and EFB at 3418, 3443, and 3387cm−1, respectively, are assigned to the stretching vibration of OH groups. The peaks at 1582, 1693, and 1576cm−1 are attributed to the CO bonds and aromatic hydrocarbons. Meanwhile, the peaks at 1356, 1384, and 1375cm−1 indicated the presence of CC bonds. The peaks at 672, 669, and 796cm−1 corresponded to the CH bond out of bending mode. The spectra of the biochar did not differ significantly from each other, suggesting that the biochar might have the same chemical-bonding structure.

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) was used to allow a direct imaging of sample on the atomic scale. Fig. 5.8 shows the HRTEM images of CD derived from coconut shell, kenaf, and EFB biochar under the same magnification. The images show a distinct shape among the three CD. Coconut-shell-based CD image shows wrinkled graphitic layers; meanwhile, the HRTEM image of kenaf-based CD displays a fibrous, tube-shaped structure. On the other hand, the HRTEM image of EFB-based CD shows a monodisperse, nearly uniform in size and sphere-shaped dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical Properties of Biochar Derived Carbon Dots
ce:section.ce:para: Photoluminescence of samples can be observed in two ways, either qualitatively or quantitatively. In qualitative method, a light source that is an ultraviolet lamp is used. When the UV radiation emitted by the light source is absorbed by the materials, the electrons are temporarily pushed to higher-energy states. The absorbed energy is then released as the electrons return to their normal state. This released energy has wavelengths in the visible light region that can be observed as fluorescent color. The fluorescent color of the CD depends on the materials used in the synthesis and the wavelength of the ultraviolet radiation (light source) that is used [15]. Fig. 5.9 shows a comparison of photoluminescence for different types of biochar at the same temperature of 250°C. Under the ambient light, both coconut-shell- and kenaf-based CD show a clear, colorless liquid, whereas EFB-based CD shows a clear, brownish liquid. Meanwhile, under the UV illumination at 365nm, it can be seen that EFB-based photoluminescent CD exhibits the brightest blue luminescence compared with the coconut-shell- and kenaf-based CD. A detailed explanation of the photoluminescence properties of the three types of biochar samples is further discussed in the next paragraph.

Quantitatively, photoluminescence spectral analysis is used as it is particularly effective in the analysis of interfaces where discrete defect and impurity states abound. An emission spectrum obtained from this spectral analysis can be described as the wavelength distribution of an emission measured at a single constant excitation wavelength [132]. In this study, CD derived from different types of biochar under the same condition temperature are characterized and compared using photoluminescence spectroscopy. Fig. 5.10 shows the photoluminescence (PL) spectrum obtained among the three types of biochar-based CD at temperature 250°C. The CD exhibits blue luminescence after being excited by a 365nm UV light source as shown in Fig. 5.9. A further observation shows that the emission peak positions for both coconut-shell- and kenaf-based CD are the same. Both spectra show strongest luminescence with emission peak at 425nm when the excitation wavelength of 300nm is used. Meanwhile, the red-shift of emission peak of EFB-based CD can be observed at 450nm with excitation wavelength of 325nm. The redshift that occurs explains the difference in brightness of luminescence between EFB-, coconut-shell-, and kenaf-based CD. These emission peaks reflect that the photoluminescence properties of the materials may originate from the similar surface oxygen groups [4].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Challenges and Future Directions in Synthesis of Carbon Dots
ce:section.ce:para: Currently, there are many available methods of preparing carbon dots using either top-down or bottom-up routes. The selection of routes to be taken depends on the starting materials chosen whether a bulk carbon sources or small precursor molecules. Although the CD has been prepared through various methods, the origin of CD mechanism is still remained elusive. According to Zhu et al., there are three main challenges regarding the PL mechanism and the solutions that can possibly be applied. First, it is a huge challenge to establish efficient and facile methods for a high-quality, mass production of CD. Since the chemical groups on the surface of CD are “uncertain,” the structure of CD is left unknown. If the chemical structure of CD can be manipulated during the synthesis, it will be possible to determine the precise mechanism. Second challenge is the insufficient characterization to highlight the PL mechanism. Due to poor investigation, most of the PL mechanism reported remains blurry. The last challenge faced is systematic comparison of all CD prepared. Even if the existing CD reported is diverse and may cause predicament on the research, the PL mechanism is actually comparable for all types of CD [100].

Some researchers even attributed the photoluminescence properties to quantum confinement effect, and the surface states are determined by the hybridization of the backbone of carbon and chemical groups attached to the carbon structure [40,133,134], but still, there is no precise theory to explain the mechanism in detail. Thus, a deeper understanding regarding its fundamental photoluminescence properties and mechanism is highly necessary. It is believed that some advanced and modified methods might play important roles in determining the mechanism. Also, a simulation of carbon source materials could be a great tool to suggest the interaction between the materials structure, properties, and photoluminescence mechanism. However, it is rarely applied in current research in which if it is employed, a more accurate model is able to determine the future application of CD.

On the contrary, a wide application of CD in industrial application may create issues related to environment and health safety. Despite the fact that CD has advantages of low toxicity and good biocompatibility, due to its high water solubility, a potential environmental release is hardly to be avoided. The accumulation of CD in the aquatic ecosystems may affect the living organisms and eventually causes harm to the human [135]. Therefore, investigations on toxicity of CD and green process in synthesizing CD are desirable.

Carbon dots have started to receive much attention after the first CD was first discovered in 2004. Since then, various syntheses either top-down or bottom-up approaches have been developed. As the semiconductor QD produced in the earlier years is considered toxigenic and mutagenic that limits its application biologically, this has inflicted the researchers to come out with a solution. The urge to find alternatives for the semiconductor QD has resulted in the explosion in the number of reports employing natural carbon sources and synthesis method within a decade. Herein, we have discussed various types of carbon sources used to synthesize CD using both top-down and bottom-up approaches. As newly emerged luminescent nanomaterials, CD has showed potentials in a wide range of applications such as photocatalysis, energy-related applications, and also sensing. This is due to its excellent properties in terms of photostability, low toxicity, and good biocompatibility and even portrayed unique optical properties.
